threats.title: VoteWeighting.removeNominee Fails to Update pointsSum Slope, Corrupting Global Emission Distribution
threats.hypothesis_description: When removeNominee is called, pointsSum[nextTime].bias is correctly reduced by the removed nominee's weight, but pointsSum[nextTime].slope is NOT adjusted to remove the nominee's slope contribution. This causes the global weight sum to decay at an incorrect rate in all subsequent weeks, systematically inflating the relative weights of remaining nominees and distorting ALL staking incentive distributions.
threats.investigation_rationale: This is a code defect in an admin-triggered function (normal operations). The slope represents how quickly the total weight decays week-over-week. After removal, the total sum bias is correct at the moment of removal but decays too fast because the slope still includes the removed nominee's voter slopes. Over time, the distortion compounds with each passing week.

proof_of_signal.pattern: pointsWeight[nomineeHash][nextTime].bias = 0 and pointsSum[nextTime].bias = newSum, but neither pointsWeight[nomineeHash][nextTime].slope nor pointsSum[nextTime].slope are updated

preconditions: Owner calls removeNominee for a nominee that has active voters with non-zero slopes
At least one week passes after removal for the corrupt slope to cause bias divergence

initial_economic_evidence.suspicious_calculation: removeNominee sets pointsSum[nextTime].bias = oldSum - oldWeight but does NOT subtract nominee's slope from pointsSum[nextTime].slope
initial_economic_evidence.manipulation_vector: After removal, _getSum() propagates the corrupt slope each week: pt.bias -= pt.slope * WEEK; where pt.slope is higher than it should be (still includes removed nominee's slope)
initial_economic_evidence.profit_hypothesis: Remaining nominees get inflated relative weights (1e18 * nomineeWeight / totalSum) because totalSum decays too fast, directing more staking OLAS inflation to them

economic_questions: How much does the total weight sum diverge per week after nominee removal?
What is the cumulative emission misdirection over typical epoch lengths?
How many nominee removals compound the distortion?
validation_requirements: Simulate removeNominee with active voters and track pointsSum evolution
Calculate relative weight inflation for remaining nominees week-by-week
Verify that revokeRemovedNomineeVotingPower only partially fixes the issue

preliminary_attack_economics.potential_profit_range: Proportional to OLAS staking emissions misdirected (could be significant)
preliminary_attack_economics.estimated_profit_margin: Depends on removed nominee's slope as fraction of total slope
preliminary_attack_economics.attack_duration: Persistent - compounds each week after removal

market_conditions_analysis.market_impact_assessment: All staking emission distributions are affected

potential_victim_impact.direct_victims: All staking targets receiving incorrectly calculated incentives
potential_victim_impact.estimated_impact_per_user: Varies based on weight distortion magnitude
potential_victim_impact.total_value_at_risk: Entire staking incentive allocation (fraction of OLAS inflation)

investigation_priorities.parameter_analysis: Typical voter slopes, number of removals, time between removals
investigation_priorities.calculation_validation: Compare theoretical vs actual pointsSum evolution after removal

threats.title: VoteWeighting.removeNominee Leaves Stale changesSum/changesWeight Entries Causing Ongoing Weight Sum Corruption
threats.hypothesis_description: When a nominee is removed, future slope change entries in changesSum[] and changesWeight[] at voters' lock expiration timestamps are NOT cleaned up. These stale entries continue to affect pointsSum when their timestamps are reached during _getSum() processing, causing ongoing corruption of the global weight sum even weeks/months after removal.
threats.investigation_rationale: While revokeRemovedNomineeVotingPower allows individual users to clean up their slope changes, this is voluntary with no enforcement. Unreovked entries persist in changesSum and will incorrectly decrement pointsSum.slope when those timestamps pass, compounding the bias distortion from ECON-H001.

proof_of_signal.pattern: removeNominee does not iterate voters to clean changesWeight[nomineeHash][voterLockEnd] and changesSum[voterLockEnd]; only revokeRemovedNomineeVotingPower (voluntary, per-user) cleans them

preconditions: Nominee has active voters whose veOLAS locks expire in the future
Voters do not call revokeRemovedNomineeVotingPower after nominee removal

initial_economic_evidence.suspicious_calculation: changesSum[voterLockEnd] still contains negative slope adjustments for removed nominee's voters
initial_economic_evidence.manipulation_vector: When _getSum() processes weeks past a voter's lock expiration, pt.slope -= changesSum[t] includes the stale decrement, making slope incorrect
initial_economic_evidence.profit_hypothesis: An attacker with veOLAS can strategically NOT revoke votes from removed nominees to keep the weight sum corrupted, benefiting their remaining voted-for nominees

economic_questions: What fraction of voters typically revoke after nominee removal?
How long do voter locks typically last after removal?
What is the net impact on pointsSum from unreovked slope changes?
validation_requirements: Simulate removal with multiple voters who don't revoke
Track changesSum entries at various future timestamps
Measure cumulative weight distortion over realistic timeframes

preliminary_attack_economics.potential_profit_range: Proportional to attacker's remaining nominee weights * emission amount
preliminary_attack_economics.estimated_profit_margin: Depends on fraction of total votes that are unrevoked
preliminary_attack_economics.attack_duration: Persists until all affected voters' locks expire

market_conditions_analysis.optimal_timing: After admin removes a highly-voted nominee
market_conditions_analysis.market_impact_assessment: Distorts emission allocation for all staking targets

potential_victim_impact.direct_victims: All staking targets - some over-allocated, some under-allocated

investigation_priorities.parameter_analysis: Typical voter lock durations, number of voters per nominee
investigation_priorities.calculation_validation: Trace changesSum evolution through _getSum iterations post-removal

threats.title: EthereumDepositProcessor Hard Reverts on Any Invalid Target, Blocking All L1 Staking Incentive Claims in Batch
threats.hypothesis_description: Unlike L2 target dispensers which gracefully withhold amounts for invalid targets, EthereumDepositProcessor._deposit hard reverts with TargetEmissionsZero if ANY single target in a batch has zero emissions limit. A deployer who disables their staking instance via setInstanceStatus can block all L1 staking incentive distributions for the same chain batch, causing a denial of service for legitimate staking targets.
threats.investigation_rationale: On L2, _processData uses a low-level call for verification and withholds on failure, continuing to process other targets. On L1, _deposit uses direct call which reverts the entire transaction. The asymmetric error handling between L1 and L2 creates a griefing vector specific to Ethereum mainnet staking targets.

proof_of_signal.pattern: if (limitAmount == 0) { revert TargetEmissionsZero(target); } — hard revert blocks entire batch, unlike L2 which uses low-level call and continues

preconditions: At least one staking target on L1 has been voted for in VoteWeighting
The deployer of that target disables it via setInstanceStatus(instance, false)
Batch claim includes the disabled target alongside legitimate targets

initial_economic_evidence.manipulation_vector: Deployer calls setInstanceStatus(instance, false) after votes are cast, making the batch claim revert for everyone
initial_economic_evidence.profit_hypothesis: Griefing attack: blocks legitimate staking targets from receiving OLAS incentives; could be weaponized by competitor staking targets

economic_questions: Can the batch be restructured to exclude the disabled target?
How long can the blocking persist (until votes expire or nominee is removed)?
What is the cost to the attacker vs the cost to victims?
validation_requirements: Confirm that sendMessageBatch from Dispenser passes all targets including zero-incentive ones after filtering
Verify the non-zero incentive filtering in _distributeStakingIncentivesBatch
Test scenario where one target disabled mid-epoch

preliminary_attack_economics.estimated_capital_needed: Cost to deploy a staking instance + some veOLAS for voting
preliminary_attack_economics.attack_duration: Until nominee is removed or votes expire

market_conditions_analysis.optimal_timing: After significant votes are accumulated for the attacker's target
market_conditions_analysis.market_impact_assessment: Blocks OLAS distribution to all L1 staking targets in the batch

potential_victim_impact.direct_victims: All legitimate L1 staking targets in the same batch claim
potential_victim_impact.estimated_impact_per_user: Complete blocking of staking incentive receipt
potential_victim_impact.total_value_at_risk: All L1 staking incentives for affected epoch claims

investigation_priorities.parameter_analysis: Number of L1 staking targets, typical batch sizes

threats.title: DefaultTargetDispenserL2.redeem Bypasses Staking Target Verification, Depositing to Potentially Invalid Contracts
threats.hypothesis_description: The redeem function allows anyone to execute queued staking deposits without re-verifying the staking target against StakingFactory.verifyInstanceAndGetEmissionsAmount. A target that was valid when originally queued (during _processData) but later became invalid (disabled by deployer, failed verifier checks) can still receive its full queued OLAS amount when redeemed.
threats.investigation_rationale: The _processData function performs verification via low-level call and queues failed deposits. When the contract becomes unpaused or receives sufficient OLAS balance, anyone can call redeem. The function only checks the queue hash exists, not whether the target is still valid.

proof_of_signal.pattern: redeem checks stakingQueueingNonces[queueHash] and OLAS balance but does NOT call stakingFactory.verifyInstanceAndGetEmissionsAmount(target)

preconditions: A staking deposit was queued during _processData (due to insufficient balance or paused state)
The staking target becomes invalid after queuing (disabled by deployer or fails updated verifier)
Sufficient OLAS balance exists in the dispenser for redemption

initial_economic_evidence.suspicious_calculation: redeem: only checks bytes32 queueHash = keccak256(abi.encode(target, amount, batchNonce)) and stakingQueueingNonces[queueHash]
initial_economic_evidence.manipulation_vector: Deployer queues deposit for their instance, disables instance, redeems queued amount to now-invalid contract
initial_economic_evidence.profit_hypothesis: OLAS deposited to an invalid/disabled staking contract where the deployer may have more control

economic_questions: What happens to OLAS deposited into a disabled staking contract?
Can the deployer extract the OLAS from their disabled contract?
What is the typical queue duration before redemption?
validation_requirements: Simulate queue→disable→redeem scenario
Check if disabled staking contracts can still receive deposits
Verify OLAS token flow after deposit to disabled contract

preliminary_attack_economics.estimated_capital_needed: Cost to deploy staking instance and get it voted for
preliminary_attack_economics.potential_profit_range: Up to the queued OLAS amount
preliminary_attack_economics.estimated_profit_margin: High if attacker controls the disabled staking contract

market_conditions_analysis.optimal_timing: After sufficient OLAS arrives on L2 to cover queued amount

potential_victim_impact.direct_victims: Protocol treasury (OLAS sent to invalid target)
potential_victim_impact.total_value_at_risk: Queued OLAS amounts on L2 dispensers

investigation_priorities.parameter_analysis: Typical queued amounts, frequency of queuing events
investigation_priorities.calculation_validation: Verify that disabled staking contracts can still accept deposit() calls

threats.title: Tokenomics.refundFromStaking Uses Wrong Address in Error Message, Potentially Masking Unauthorized Callers
threats.hypothesis_description: The refundFromStaking function checks that msg.sender == dispenser but the error message references the depository address: revert ManagerOnly(msg.sender, depository). This is a demonstrable code defect where the error output does not match the actual check, making it harder to diagnose unauthorized access attempts and potentially confusing integrators.
threats.investigation_rationale: The access control logic itself is correct (checks dispenser), but the error message misleads by reporting depository as the required manager. This is a code correctness issue that could delay debugging during incident response.

proof_of_signal.pattern: Checks msg.sender != dispenser but reverts with ManagerOnly(msg.sender, depository) — error references wrong address variable

preconditions: Any unauthorized caller attempts refundFromStaking

initial_economic_evidence.manipulation_vector: Misleading error output; the check is correct but the revert message is wrong
initial_economic_evidence.profit_hypothesis: No direct profit — code correctness defect

economic_questions: Could the misleading error cause operational confusion during an incident?
Are there other instances of similar error message mismatches?
validation_requirements: Verify the access check logic is correct despite wrong error message
Confirm no other functions have similar mismatches

preliminary_attack_economics.potential_profit_range: N/A — code defect, no direct profit

investigation_priorities.calculation_validation: Confirm correct variable is used in check vs error

threats.title: Tokenomics.checkpoint Lacks Reentrancy Guard Despite External Call to Treasury Before Epoch State Update
threats.hypothesis_description: The checkpoint function makes an external call to ITreasury(treasury).rebalanceTreasury(incentives[1]) before incrementing epochCounter. The _locked reentrancy variable exists in the contract but is NOT checked in checkpoint(). If the treasury contract were compromised or malicious, it could re-enter tokenomics functions (e.g., refundFromStaking) during the call, corrupting epoch state.
threats.investigation_rationale: The checkpoint function modifies critical state (staking points, effective bond) and then makes an external call before the final epochCounter increment. A malicious treasury callback to refundFromStaking would modify mapEpochStakingPoints[eCounter].stakingIncentive AFTER it was already set, creating inconsistency between the emitted event and storage values.

proof_of_signal.pattern: ITreasury(treasury).rebalanceTreasury(incentives[1]) called before epochCounter = uint32(eCounter + 1); no _locked check in checkpoint

preconditions: Treasury contract is compromised or malicious (set by owner)
Malicious treasury calls back into refundFromStaking during rebalanceTreasury

initial_economic_evidence.suspicious_calculation: mapEpochStakingPoints[eCounter].stakingIncentive set, then treasury call, then epochCounter incremented
initial_economic_evidence.manipulation_vector: Reentrant call to refundFromStaking modifies mapEpochStakingPoints[eCounter].stakingIncentive after checkpoint already set it
initial_economic_evidence.profit_hypothesis: Could inflate staking incentives for current epoch beyond intended inflation schedule

economic_questions: Can the treasury actually re-enter through refundFromStaking (msg.sender must be dispenser)?
What state is vulnerable between rebalanceTreasury call and epochCounter increment?
Could a compromised treasury redirect funds?
validation_requirements: Map all state that is modified before vs after the external call
Verify if any reentrant path can bypass access controls
Test reentrancy scenario with mock malicious treasury

preliminary_attack_economics.estimated_capital_needed: Requires compromising treasury contract (admin key theft)

market_conditions_analysis.market_impact_assessment: Could corrupt entire epoch's incentive calculations

potential_victim_impact.total_value_at_risk: One epoch's worth of OLAS inflation

investigation_priorities.parameter_analysis: State variables modified before vs after rebalanceTreasury call
investigation_priorities.calculation_validation: Test reentrancy paths from treasury callback

threats.title: Permissionless Nominee Addition Enables L1 Batch Claim Griefing via Unverified Staking Targets
threats.hypothesis_description: Anyone can add a nominee to VoteWeighting without verification that the address is a valid staking contract. If an attacker adds a non-factory-deployed address as a nominee for L1 chain Id and accumulates enough votes, attempting to claim staking incentives for that target on L1 triggers a hard revert in EthereumDepositProcessor._deposit (TargetEmissionsZero). Combined with batch claiming, this blocks incentive distribution for all L1 targets in the same batch.
threats.investigation_rationale: VoteWeighting.addNomineeEVM is permissionless by design. The protocol documentation confirms this. While L2 dispensers handle invalid targets gracefully (withholding), the L1 EthereumDepositProcessor reverts entirely. An attacker with veOLAS can vote for the invalid nominee and combine with the L1 batch revert to grief legitimate targets.

proof_of_signal.pattern: addNomineeEVM has no access control (permissionless) and does not verify nominee is a valid staking contract

preconditions: Attacker has veOLAS to vote for the invalid nominee
Invalid nominee is added for the L1 chain Id (block.chainid)
Batch claim includes the invalid nominee alongside valid L1 targets
WEIGHT_VOTE_DELAY (10 days) must pass between votes

initial_economic_evidence.suspicious_calculation: addNomineeEVM accepts any non-zero address as nominee without factory verification
initial_economic_evidence.manipulation_vector: Attacker adds random address as nominee, votes for it, batch claim for L1 reverts
initial_economic_evidence.profit_hypothesis: No direct profit; griefing attack blocks legitimate L1 staking incentive distribution

economic_questions: How much veOLAS does the attacker need to make the invalid nominee have non-zero staking incentive?
Can legitimate claimers exclude the invalid target from their batch?
How quickly can the DAO remove the invalid nominee?
validation_requirements: Verify that claimStakingIncentivesBatch for L1 chain routes through EthereumDepositProcessor
Test batch claim with one invalid and one valid L1 target
Confirm individual claim (non-batch) for L1 chain also reverts

preliminary_attack_economics.estimated_capital_needed: veOLAS lock (>= minStakingWeight worth of voting power)
preliminary_attack_economics.attack_duration: Until nominee removed by owner + 1 epoch grace period

market_conditions_analysis.optimal_timing: When significant L1 staking incentives are pending

potential_victim_impact.estimated_impact_per_user: Delayed or blocked staking incentive receipt
potential_victim_impact.total_value_at_risk: All L1 staking incentives for affected epochs

investigation_priorities.parameter_analysis: Minimum veOLAS needed, typical L1 staking targets count
investigation_priorities.calculation_validation: End-to-end trace from vote to L1 deposit processor revert

threats.title: StakingBase Checkpoint Reward Rounding Systematically Favors First Eligible Service in Array
threats.hypothesis_description: When totalRewards exceeds lastAvailableRewards in the checkpoint function, rewards are proportionally scaled down. The loop processes services from index 1 first, then handles service[0] last, assigning any rounding dust (lastAvailableRewards - updatedTotalRewards) to it. The first eligible service consistently receives extra wei from rounding. While individually tiny, this is a systematic code defect in reward distribution fairness.
threats.investigation_rationale: The code explicitly processes service[0] separately and adds any leftover dust to it. This creates a deterministic advantage for whichever service occupies position 0 in the eligibleServiceIds array. Over many checkpoints, the dust accumulates for the same service.

proof_of_signal.pattern: Loop starts from i=1, processes service[0] last with dust addition: if (lastAvailableRewards > updatedTotalRewards) { updatedReward += lastAvailableRewards - updatedTotalRewards; }

preconditions: totalRewards > lastAvailableRewards (proportional scaling path)
Multiple eligible services exist
Rounding creates non-zero dust (common with integer division)

initial_economic_evidence.suspicious_calculation: updatedReward = (eligibleServiceRewards[i] * lastAvailableRewards) / totalRewards creates rounding dust
initial_economic_evidence.manipulation_vector: First service in array always receives the rounding residual
initial_economic_evidence.profit_hypothesis: Dust per checkpoint is small (1-N wei where N = numServices), but accumulates over many checkpoints

economic_questions: What is the maximum dust amount per checkpoint?
How is the order of eligibleServiceIds determined?
Can a service manipulate its position in the array?
validation_requirements: Calculate typical dust amounts for realistic scenarios
Verify eligibleServiceIds ordering matches setServiceIds ordering
Assess cumulative impact over hundreds of checkpoints

preliminary_attack_economics.estimated_capital_needed: Cost of deploying and staking a service

investigation_priorities.calculation_validation: Simulate proportional scaling with realistic values

threats.title: Dispenser.claimOwnerIncentives Reverts When Zero Incentives Accumulated Instead of Returning Gracefully
threats.hypothesis_description: When a component/agent owner calls claimOwnerIncentives with valid unit IDs but has accumulated zero rewards and zero top-ups, the function reverts with ClaimIncentivesFailed because the success variable remains false (never set to true when reward+topUp == 0). This is a code defect that causes unnecessary reverts for legitimate users who may not know their balance beforehand.
threats.investigation_rationale: The function only sets success=true inside the (reward+topUp) > 0 branch via treasury.withdrawToAccount. When both are zero, success stays false, and the final check reverts. This forces users to always pre-check their balances via getOwnerIncentives before claiming, and any integration that calls claimOwnerIncentives without pre-checking will fail.

proof_of_signal.pattern: success = ITreasury(treasury).withdrawToAccount(...) only called when (reward + topUp) > 0; when both are zero, success remains false, triggering revert ClaimIncentivesFailed

preconditions: User has valid unit IDs but zero accumulated rewards and top-ups
This can occur if no donations were made to their service, or if fractions were set to zero

initial_economic_evidence.suspicious_calculation: bool success; /* false by default */ ... if ((reward + topUp) > 0) { success = treasury.withdrawToAccount(...); } if (!success) { revert; }
initial_economic_evidence.manipulation_vector: Not exploitable for profit; code defect causing unnecessary reverts
initial_economic_evidence.profit_hypothesis: No profit — UX/integration issue causing transaction failures

economic_questions: How often do users call claim with zero balances?
Are there integrations that batch-claim without pre-checking?
validation_requirements: Confirm revert path when both reward and topUp are zero
Check if any protocol integrations depend on non-reverting claim behavior

market_conditions_analysis.market_impact_assessment: Transaction failures for users with zero balances

potential_victim_impact.direct_victims: Users calling claim without pre-checking balances

investigation_priorities.calculation_validation: Trace execution path when reward=0 and topUp=0

threats.title: Tokenomics Donation Amount Truncation Causes Per-Unit Reward Loss While Epoch Total Remains Inflated
threats.hypothesis_description: In _trackServiceDonations, the donation amount per unit is calculated as uint96(amounts[i] / numServiceUnits), losing the remainder to integer truncation. However, the full donation amount is already added to totalDonationsETH. This creates a discrepancy where the epoch's total recorded donations exceed the sum of what is actually allocated to units, causing the IDF and treasury reward calculations to use an inflated base.
threats.investigation_rationale: The remainder amounts[i] % numServiceUnits is lost per service per donation. These lost wei are never allocated to any unit's pendingRelativeReward or pendingRelativeTopUp, yet they inflate totalDonationsETH. For services with many units and small donation amounts, the truncation could be non-trivial in aggregate.

proof_of_signal.pattern: uint96 amount = uint96(amounts[i] / numServiceUnits); — truncates remainder; totalDonationsETH includes full amounts[i] (line 605)

preconditions: Donation amounts are not perfectly divisible by number of service units
Services with many units receive small donations

initial_economic_evidence.suspicious_calculation: amount = amounts[i] / numServiceUnits truncates; remainder = amounts[i] % numServiceUnits lost but counted in totalDonationsETH
initial_economic_evidence.manipulation_vector: Creating services with many units to maximize truncation
initial_economic_evidence.profit_hypothesis: Treasury reward fraction is computed on inflated totalDonationsETH: treasuryRewards = (totalDonationsETH * treasuryFraction) / 100

economic_questions: What is the maximum truncation per donation call?
Does the inflated totalDonationsETH cause treasury to claim more than actually donated?
Can this be exploited to create accounting discrepancies?
validation_requirements: Calculate typical truncation amounts for realistic service configurations
Verify that treasury actually receives the inflated amount vs actual unit allocations
Check if the IDF is materially affected by truncation

preliminary_attack_economics.potential_profit_range: Very small per transaction (up to numUnits-1 wei per service per donation)

market_conditions_analysis.market_impact_assessment: Minimal per-transaction; could compound over many donations

potential_victim_impact.direct_victims: Unit owners lose truncated donation amounts
potential_victim_impact.total_value_at_risk: Negligible individually; could accumulate over protocol lifetime

investigation_priorities.calculation_validation: Compare totalDonationsETH vs sum of per-unit allocations
